text: Clear sky tonight? Point low at the eastern horizon after 9. The steady dot that doesn't twinkle is Jupiter. With the 25mm eyepiece you'll catch up to four of its moons in a tidy little line.
Blurry Moon? Two fixes, in this order. Swap to the 25mm eyepiece and center it, then nudge focus slowly past sharp and back. The crisp point is narrow and easy to overshoot.
Drove an hour for dark sky and the clouds rolled in at 9:58. We still caught fifteen minutes on the Pleiades through the gap. Faint counts.
We ship a 25mm eyepiece as the default, not a high-power one. Wide and bright first means you actually find the thing. The 10mm is in the box for when you're ready to zoom in.
The red-dot finder isn't aligned out of the box. Center a bright star in the eyepiece, then move the red dot onto it. Two minutes, and every hunt after gets easier.
Cold night? Let the scope sit outside for twenty minutes before you expect a crisp image. Warm glass and cold air make the view shimmer.
First time my kid found Saturn's rings on their own. The pause, then the quiet 'whoa.' That's the whole reason we built this.
Light pollution ate Andromeda last night, so we scanned slowly off the corner of the Square of Pegasus with the 25mm. A faint smudge two and a half million light-years out. Found it anyway.
The ST-1 is a tabletop scope on purpose. Tripods are where a lot of beginners quit. Set it on a picnic table and you're observing in a minute.
A sliver Moon tonight, which is exactly why the craters look best. Low sun angle along the terminator throws long shadows you can trace.
Three minutes from box to a sharp Moon: tabletop down, red-dot on the Moon, 25mm in, focus slow. Then swap to the 10mm once it's centered.
Orion is up. The fuzzy 'star' in the middle of the sword is the Orion Nebula. Low power, let your eye settle, and the wings start to show.
We left a streak-counter out of the app on purpose. We didn't want the hobby to feel like a chore you owe. Look up when the sky is good, not because a badge is waiting.
Some nights the sky just doesn't cooperate. That's the hobby, not a failure. We went back two nights later and the Double Cluster was waiting.
The 'tonight' feed answers one question: what should I point at right now? No charts to read. Tap the top card, follow the finder, look.
Why a red dot and not a little finder-scope? A red dot shows the real sky at 1x, so you point with both eyes open. Finder-scopes flip the view and confuse beginners.
Saturn is low in the south after dark this month. Even at 25mm the rings read as 'ears.' Bump to the 10mm and they separate into a clean ring.
Collimation sounds scary. For the ST-1 it's a thirty-second check with the card in the box: look down the tube, line up the dots, done. We'll post the close-up.
Backyard, too much city glow for Andromeda, tried anyway. The faint counts. Drag the scope ten feet into the shadow of the house and the contrast jumps.
The Pleiades look better at low power than high. The whole little dipper of blue stars fits in the 25mm field. Crank the magnification and you lose the cluster.
App tip: save what you find. The log isn't for streaks, it's so a cloudy week doesn't erase the night you finally caught Jupiter's moons.
You don't need a dark-sky trip for the Moon. The Moon is the one object that laughs at light pollution. Start there your first clear night.
The terminator — the line between lunar day and night — is where the detail lives. Aim there, not at the bright full face, for the sharpest craters.
Took the ST-1 camping. Set it on the cooler lid, aligned the red dot in a minute, and three kids took turns on Jupiter until the batteries on the lantern died.
If the image jumps every time you touch the focus, that's the table, not the scope. A heavier surface or a hand on the base steadies it instantly.
We get asked why the ST-1 isn't motorized. Motors mean batteries, setup menus, and a price jump. For finding your first hundred objects, your hand and the red dot are faster.
The Double Cluster in Perseus is two tight knots of stars in the same field. It's our favorite 'I didn't know my scope could do that' object for a first month.
Dew on the eyepiece? Don't wipe it. Cap it, point the tube down for a few minutes, and let it clear. Wiping smears and scratches over a season.
Summer Milky Way season: from a dark site, just sweep the 25mm slowly along the band. You don't need a target. Star fields for twenty minutes is its own reward.
A new owner's first clear night should end in a real object, not a manual. That's the whole design brief: box open to 'whoa' in under five minutes.
The 10mm eyepiece is for when you've already found the thing at 25mm. Center first, then zoom. Zoom first and you'll spend the night hunting a black field.
Mars is small even in big scopes. At the ST-1 it's a bright, steady orange dot — and that's honest. We'd rather you know than chase detail that isn't there.
Bring a red flashlight, not your phone. White light wipes out your night vision for twenty minutes; red keeps it. The app has a red mode for exactly this.
Cloudy forecast all week. Good time to practice the red-dot alignment indoors on a far streetlight so it's muscle memory when the sky clears.
The honest truth about a 130mm scope: galaxies are faint smudges, not the photos. But the Moon, planets, and bright clusters are genuinely stunning live. Set expectations and you'll love it.
We built the first-light walkthrough because we kept watching people give up at 'now what.' Six taps: pick tonight's object, align, find, focus, look, save.

attestation.reason: Canonical product-positioning line, authored in-house for the fixture; safe to reuse verbatim.

text: Astronomy, not astrology. We will never tell you what the stars mean for your week. We'll tell you which one is a planet and how to point at it.

attestation.reason: Brand-voice north-star line; intentionally reusable as a values statement.

text: Three minutes to a sharp Moon, every time: tabletop, red-dot on, 25mm in, focus slow, then 10mm. Pinned because new owners ask this most.

attestation.reason: Most-requested onboarding answer; curated as an evergreen reply template.

text: Clear sky, no idea where to start? Open the app, tap the top 'tonight' card, follow the finder. The feed always has an answer to 'now what do I look at.'
First light is the whole hobby in one moment: the first time the thing you pointed at turns out to be real. Everything we make is in service of that one minute.
